statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Input is a single integer n, representing the size of the chess board.
statement.outputFormat: Output is the count of board configurations with no attacks between queens.
statement.notes: Write a function that counts the number of solutions for the N queens puzzle. In this puzzle, n queens are placed on an n x n chessboard in such a way that no two queens attack each other.

examples.explanation: There is exactly 1 way to place 1 queen on a 1x1 board.
There are no valid ways to place 2 queens on a 2x2 board since they would attack each other.
There are 10 valid ways to place 5 queens on a 5x5 board.

hints.text: Understand the N-Queens problem, and how a chessboard can be represented as a boolean matrix.

editorial.title: Backtracking: N-Queens II
editorial.intuition: To solve the N-Queens puzzle, we can use backtracking to place queens on the board. We need to find a way to represent a valid board configuration and a way to check if two queens are attacking each other.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to generate all possible board configurations and check if they are valid.
editorial.betterSolution: We can improve the solution by using backtracking and a boolean matrix to represent the board.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses backtracking and a boolean matrix to represent the board. We recursively place queens on the board and use a boolean matrix to check if two queens are attacking each other.
commonMistakes: Not checking if two queens are attacking each other.
Not using a boolean matrix to represent the board.
